title: Chapter 1: Ash and Flame
paragraphs: The world screamed. It wasn't a sound, but a physical sensation of pressure in the temples, a maddening, continuous echo of torn matter. The once-blooming plains that breathed with life had turned into a colossal mass grave, covered with a thick layer of radioactive ash. It creaked underfoot like ground glass, lunged into the lungs and settled on armor like a gray, dead crust. The wind brought no coolness—it bore only the stifling, nauseating smell of rotting flesh, ozone, and molten copper.

On the summit of a broken basalt cliff, as motionless as an ancient effigy of sorrow, stood Avotu. Heavy armor, covered with century-old dents and dried black blood, weighed on his shoulders, but this physical heaviness was insignificant compared to the lead that lay on his soul. His head was crowned not by a helmet or hair—above the pale face of the fire elf lived and writhed an absolutely black, light-absorbing flame. It did not warm; it greedily absorbed the reflection of the dying reality, chilled in moments of despair and soared into the sky in minutes of blind rage.

His eyes—two bottomless pits in which night had forever frozen—watched the east with painful unshakeable intensity. The air there rippled, trembled like the haze over a red-hot furnace. Space stretched to its limit and burst with the vile, wet sound of tearing meat. Another festering wound in the heavens. A portal.
A nightmare poured from the crimson, bleeding hole. Grotesque, multi-jointed creatures of the void with scythe-like limbs descended upon the ground, splashing acidic saliva that melted stone.

With a roar like the moan of a dying leviathan, pillars of hellfire struck from beneath the rocks. The warriors of the demon Zakhan—lowly, spiked-chitin creatures—rushed at the newcomers. A dull, visceral meat grinder ensued. The screech of tearing metal, the crunch of chitin, the gurgling of black blood, instantly boiling on the remnants of mana. They tore each other to pieces, feeling neither fear nor pity.

Avotu watched this carnage with an icy void in his chest. His hand in a heavy plate gauntlet mechanically tightened on the worn hilt of the Nature's Blade. The meteoric iron responded with a familiar, almost living vibration. If there were even one innocent in that valley, Avotu would have thrown himself into the epicenter, crushing skulls and burning flesh. But there was no one to save. The world had become an arena for monsters.
The whistle of the wind suddenly took on a familiar tone. And Avotu fell into hell. Again.

A panic attack struck without warning, professionally and mercilessly. The elf's lungs reflexively constricted, refusing to take in air. The black flame on his head dimmed, shrinking to miserable embers. Cool greenery of an ancient forest bloomed before his glazed eyes. He smelled the needles and heard laughter. Biotrix. Her blonde hair tickled his cheek, her green eyes glowed with infinite trust and love.

And then the heavens split. In that same second, Avotu felt Biotrix's fingers slip out of his palm. He reached for her, tearing his voice in a silent scream, while the gravitational anomaly of the void lifted her fragile body into the air... and tore it in half, generously splashing scarlet blood on his face.

Avotu fell to his knees directly into the gray ash, convulsively swallowing the poisoned air. He clawed his gauntlets into his own shoulders, trying to anchor himself with pain so as not to lose his mind. Guilt, ancient and all-consuming, corroded him from within.
'I failed to hold you,' his thought beat in agony, drowning out the sounds of the carnage below. 'I am the most powerful warrior of my era, and I am absolutely, totally useless.'
The fire elf, feared by kings and demons alike, sobbed without tears on the edge of the abyss, unable to forgive himself for outliving her.

A hundred leagues to the north, in the infinitely black and oppressive catacombs of a collapsed city, the silence was also saturated with terror. Claustrophobia squeezed the walls, making five people instinctively huddle close to each other.

They sat tightly, shoulder to shoulder, feeling the shivers of their friends. No mockery, no reproaches. Here, in the darkness of an alien, hostile dimension, the only light was their bond, forged back home.
Evgeniy breathed heavily, clutching a piece of heavy concrete rebar with both hands. Panic crawled through his veins like icy poison.

Yuri, sitting on the right, gently placed his hand on his friend's twitching shoulder. The man whose life had once consisted of regulations and paper bureaucracy had encountered the complete collapse of logic. But he held on.
'Breathe, Zheka,' Yuri whispered, barely controlling the tremor in his own voice. 'We're together. We'll get out. We have to.'

Anar fortified himself on the left. He was always characterized by a lazy indifference to everything in the world, except for these people. He sat cross-legged, clutching the blade of a broken utility knife. An absolute concentration was read in his dilated pupils. If a creature leaped from the darkness, he wouldn't run—he would throw himself directly at it to buy seconds for the rest.

Ravan sat slightly ahead, covering the group with his huge muscular frame. The dentist, accustomed to the whiteness of his clinic and the smile of his little son in the evenings, understood the insignificance of his physical strength in this darkness. But he was ready to rip throats with his teeth to protect his 'pack'.
Alexander, their unspoken leader, slowly looked out from behind a fallen column, clutching the shaft of an old, rusty crossbow.

'It's walking above,' Sasha said barely audibly, turning to his friends. His face was pale but determined. 'Guys, listen to me. We don't know where we've been thrown. We don't know the rules. But we won't part. If we strike—we strike together. If we run—we run together.'

Five people, plucked from a warm apartment directly into a festering hell, responded with a synchronous, confident nod. The vaults of the dungeon shuddered from the heavy footsteps above. The horror of this world tried to break them, but as long as they remembered who was sitting nearby, their humanity remained an impregnable fortress.
